result:
There is a peculiar kind of holiness in the unremarkable hours. We spend so much of our lives waiting for the crescendo—the promotion, the wedding, the departure, the arrival—that we miss the quiet sacraments happening around us every afternoon. The way sunlight falls through kitchen windows at four o'clock, turning dust motes into constellations. The sound of a neighbor's wind chimes carrying across the fence on a breeze we cannot see. The satisfying click of a door closing at exactly the right angle, or the particular weight of a cat settling against your leg.

I used to believe that meaning accumulated only in the peaks, those rarefied moments of intense joy or grief that split our lives into Before and After. But I'm learning that grace lives in the valleys too. It lives in the steam rising from a mug of tea while rain paints the glass grey. It lives in the comfortable silence between old friends who have run out of things to say but haven't run out of wanting to be near each other. It lives in the smell of bread cooling on a wire rack, or the precise shade of blue the sky turns just before dusk.

These moments ask nothing of us. They don't require planning or preparation, performance or proof, only our attention. They are the ballast that keeps us steady when the earth tilts unexpectedly. And perhaps that's the real miracle—not that extraordinary things sometimes happen to us, but that ordinary things happen constantly, offering themselves like gifts we forgot we already possessed, waiting in the quiet light of a Tuesday.
